---
title: "Energy, power, efficiency, and heat"
description: "Trace electrical energy through a system, close power and efficiency budgets, and turn dissipation into a defensible thermal and component-rating decision."
chapter-id: F05
status: draft
---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity — draft"}
This chapter presents a complete instructional path: signed power and energy
derivations, DC and pulsed examples, a system budget, executable arithmetic,
explicitly synthetic thermal data, authentic manufacturer specifications, a
failure diagnosis, and a guarded design decision. It remains subject to technical
and editorial revision. The synthetic data teach analysis but do not qualify a
physical assembly; physical evidence belongs in aligned Lab **L04**. See the
[reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) for the meaning of status levels.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary for energy and thermal work"}
Any practical work discussed here is limited to **current-limited,
extra-low-voltage** sources, approved components, local supervision, and an
approved procedure. A low voltage can still cause burns, fire, component rupture,
or battery damage when the available current or stored energy is high. Components
can remain hot after power is removed; never use touch as a thermometer. De-energize
before changing the circuit, set a conservative current limit before connecting,
and stop for unexpected odor, sound, discoloration, swelling, smoke, unstable
current, or temperature. This chapter is not authorization for mains, high voltage,
high-energy cells or packs, RF power, rotating machines, medical equipment, or
safety-critical systems [@iec61010].
:::

## Central question

> Where does energy enter, move through, and leave an electronic system?

Imagine an **illustrative**, current-limited 5.00 V assembly. At its input test
points it draws 120 mA after temperatures and readings settle. It supplies a
3.30 V, 100 mA load and a 5.00 V, 10 mA indicator branch. The output voltages are
correct, yet one conversion component becomes warm.

Before calculating, predict:

- Is charge being “used up,” or is energy being transferred and converted?
- Does the 3.30 V load receive all $5.00~\text{V}\times120~\text{mA}$?
- Which number describes an amount: joules, watts, or degrees Celsius?
- If the component dissipates the same power in a warmer room, what happens to
  its final temperature?
- Does a case temperature below a datasheet limit prove that an inaccessible
  internal hot spot is below its limit?

The electrical readings alone tell only part of the story. A complete account
needs declared directions, a time interval, a system boundary, and a heat-flow
path. The physical assembly, schematic, equations, code, temperature record, and
acceptance rule must describe that same account.

## Learning outcomes

This chapter assumes [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd): associated voltage
and current references, sources and loads, resistance, operating points, and
component ratings. Algebra and SI support are available in
[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd). After completing this chapter,
you should be able to:

- derive signed instantaneous power from voltage, charge, and current, and
  interpret positive and negative results under the passive sign convention;
- distinguish energy, instantaneous power, average power, RMS heating, stored
  energy, dissipation, temperature, and heat transfer;
- place a boundary around a DC or periodic system, account for accumulation, and
  close an auditable power and efficiency budget without double-counting;
- use dissipation, ambient temperature, thermal resistance, and thermal
  capacitance within their stated limits to predict steady temperature rise and
  settling behavior;
- read power, temperature, voltage, and pulse ratings as simultaneous conditional
  limits rather than interchangeable targets; and
- plan measurements and apply a guarded decision rule that distinguishes a
  nominal feasibility estimate from verified thermal performance.

The thermal step response can be used and checked with algebra and an exponential;
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd) is just-in-time support
only for its optional differential-equation derivation.

The chapter proceeds in the same order as an engineering decision: establish the
sign of terminal power, accumulate energy over time, close a system boundary,
define useful output and efficiency, predict temperature rise, and finally compare
the result with conditional ratings and a guarded acceptance rule.

## Terminal power and the passive sign convention

Begin with a two-terminal element. Name its terminals A and B and define the
instantaneous voltage

$$
v(t)\equiv V_A(t)-V_B(t),
$$

placing $+$ at A and $-$ at B. Define current $i(t)$ as positive **entering A**, the
terminal marked $+$. These are associated references: the **passive sign
convention**. Lowercase symbols denote instantaneous quantities; steady DC values
will use uppercase symbols [@sedra2020microelectronic].

Voltage is energy transferred per unit charge. With the stated polarity, an
increment of positive charge $\mathrm dq$ traversing the element from A to B
corresponds to incremental energy absorbed by the element

$$
\mathrm dW_\text{abs}=v(t)\,\mathrm dq.
$$

Current is the rate of charge transfer, $i(t)=\mathrm dq/\mathrm dt$. Dividing the
energy increment by time gives the **definition of instantaneous absorbed power**:

$$
p_\text{abs}(t)
\equiv\frac{\mathrm dW_\text{abs}}{\mathrm dt}
=v(t)\frac{\mathrm dq}{\mathrm dt}
=v(t)i(t).
$$ {#eq-f05-instantaneous-power}

The dimensional chain is

$$
[vi]=\text{V}\cdot\text{A}
=\frac{\text{J}}{\text{C}}\frac{\text{C}}{\text{s}}
=\frac{\text{J}}{\text{s}}=\text{W}.
$$

The watt (W) is one joule per second; it is a **rate**, not an amount of energy
[@bipm2019si]. Under the declared references:

- $p_\text{abs}>0$: the element absorbs electrical energy at that instant;
- $p_\text{abs}<0$: the element delivers electrical energy at that instant; and
- $p_\text{abs}=0$: no net terminal energy transfer occurs at that instant,
  although the element may still contain stored energy.

A negative result is not “negative physics.” It says the actual transfer is
opposite to absorption under the selected references. If only current is reversed,
$i'=-i$ no longer enters the voltage-positive terminal and absorbed power must be
written $p_\text{abs}=-vi'$. Reversing voltage alone similarly gives
$p_\text{abs}=-v'i$. Reversing both restores associated references and
$p_\text{abs}=v'i'$. The physical transfer does not change when an arrow or
polarity mark is redrawn.

::: {.callout-note title="Signed source and load — one physical transfer"}
Place 5.00 V DC across an illustrative 100 Ω ohmic load. Define $I_L$ entering the
load's positive terminal. Then

$$
I_L=\frac{5.00~\text{V}}{100~\Omega}=50.0~\text{mA},
\qquad
P_{L,\text{abs}}=(5.00~\text{V})(50.0~\text{mA})=+0.250~\text{W}.
$$

For the source, keep its voltage positive at the terminal from which current
leaves, but define its associated current $I_S$ as entering that positive terminal.
The physical current therefore gives $I_S=-50.0$ mA and

$$
P_{S,\text{abs}}=(5.00~\text{V})(-50.0~\text{mA})=-0.250~\text{W}.
$$

The signed terminal powers sum to zero. Over 60.0 s the load absorbs 15.0 J and
the source delivers 15.0 J. Ideal wires add no loss in this first calculation.
Real source resistance, leads, and contacts would add positive absorbed powers,
reducing what reaches the load.
:::

This terminal description does not mean charge carriers race from the source to
the load carrying packets of energy. Charge continuity and electromagnetic energy
transfer are distinct descriptions; F02 established their boundary. Here the
terminal product $vi$ is the correct lumped quantity for accounting.

## Energy and average power over an interval

Power answers “how fast now?” Energy answers “how much over an interval?” For a
fixed interval from $t_0$ to $t_1$, summing small power-times-duration rectangles
and taking their limiting value gives

$$
\Delta W_\text{abs}
=\int_{t_0}^{t_1}p_\text{abs}(t)\,\mathrm dt
=\int_{t_0}^{t_1}v(t)i(t)\,\mathrm dt.
$$ {#eq-f05-energy-integral}

The **average power over that named interval** is defined as

$$
P_\text{avg}
\equiv\frac{1}{t_1-t_0}
\int_{t_0}^{t_1}p(t)\,\mathrm dt
=\frac{\Delta W}{t_1-t_0}.
$$ {#eq-f05-average-power}

For constant DC power, @eq-f05-energy-integral reduces exactly to
$\Delta W=P\Delta t$. A 0.250 W load operated for 60.0 s absorbs 15.0 J. Operated
for 1 h, it absorbs 0.250 Wh. The conversion

$$
1~\text{Wh}=(1~\text{J/s})(3600~\text{s})=3600~\text{J}
$$

is exact. A watt-hour is energy; a watt is power. “Watts per hour” means a rate of
change of power and is not a substitute for either.

Battery capacity in ampere-hours is charge, because
$1~\text{Ah}=3600~\text{C}$. Multiplying an ampere-hour rating by a representative
terminal voltage gives only an **energy estimate**. For example, an illustrative
2.0 Ah cell at a nominal 3.7 V suggests $7.4$ Wh, but usable energy depends on its
voltage curve, discharge rate, temperature, aging, cutoff, and converter losses.
Use a manufacturer discharge specification for a design.

### Mode-weighted energy budgets

Many electronic systems alternate among operating modes rather than draw one
constant power. Suppose mode $k$ has approximately constant absorbed power $P_k$
for duration $t_k$ during a repeated observation interval. Applying
@eq-f05-energy-integral to each interval gives

$$
E_\text{interval}=\sum_k P_k t_k,
\qquad
P_\text{avg}=\frac{\sum_k P_k t_k}{\sum_k t_k}
=\sum_k D_kP_k,
$$ {#eq-f05-mode-budget}

where $D_k\equiv t_k/\sum_jt_j$ is the dimensionless duty fraction and
$\sum_kD_k=1$. This relation is exact for the stated piecewise-constant powers.
Transition energy, startup surges, and modes omitted from the inventory must be
added separately.

For an illustrative 5.00 V sensor, define input current as positive into the
system. It draws 150 mA for 10 ms and 10.0 mA for the remaining 990 ms of each
second. The active duty fraction is 0.010, so

$$
P_\text{avg}
=(0.010)(5.00~\text{V})(150~\text{mA})
+(0.990)(5.00~\text{V})(10.0~\text{mA})
=57.0~\text{mW}.
$$

Over 24.0 h the predicted input energy is
$(57.0~\text{mW})(24.0~\text{h})=1.37$ Wh. The 0.750 W active peak sets source,
wiring, and transient requirements; the much smaller mode-weighted average sets
the first energy and steady-heating estimate. A meter that misses the 10 ms event
can understate the peak even when its long-term average current is adequate.

### Average and RMS power for pulsed waveforms

For a constant, positive, ohmic resistance $R$, with the passive sign convention,
$v(t)=Ri(t)$. Substitution into @eq-f05-instantaneous-power gives the constitutive
results

$$
p_R(t)=i^2(t)R=\frac{v^2(t)}{R}\ge0.
$$ {#eq-f05-resistor-power}

These forms are valid while $R$ is adequately constant. Self-heating,
temperature coefficient, voltage coefficient, non-ohmic behavior, and pulses
large or long enough to change temperature or operating region can break that
assumption.

Now apply a 10.0 V rectangular pulse to an illustrative 100 Ω resistor for 25 %
of every period, with zero voltage otherwise. During each on-time,
$i=100$ mA and $p=1.00$ W. Over a full period,

$$
P_\text{avg}=(0.25)(1.00~\text{W})+(0.75)(0)=0.250~\text{W}.
$$

Yet $V_\text{avg}=2.50$ V and $I_\text{avg}=25.0$ mA, whose product is only
62.5 mW. In general,

$$
\operatorname{avg}(vi)\ne
\operatorname{avg}(v)\operatorname{avg}(i).
$$

The **RMS** value is defined to preserve heating equivalence in a constant
resistance:

$$
I_\text{rms}
\equiv\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i^2(t)\,\mathrm dt},
\qquad
P_{R,\text{avg}}=I_\text{rms}^2R.
$$ {#eq-f05-rms-heating}

For the pulse, $I_\text{rms}=100~\text{mA}\sqrt{0.25}=50.0$ mA and
$V_\text{rms}=5.00$ V, reproducing 0.250 W. Even then,
$V_\text{rms}I_\text{rms}$ is not universally average power for arbitrary loads;
voltage and current timing must be correlated. F08 develops sinusoidal phase,
apparent power, and power factor.

An average-power pass also does not prove a pulse pass. Peak voltage, peak
current, energy per pulse, pulse duration, repetition rate, and thermal impedance
may impose separate limits.

## Energy conservation across a system boundary

Draw a boundary around the device or system being studied. Name every port that
crosses it and choose one of two accounting languages:

1. **Signed terminal powers:** use the passive sign convention at every port and
   sum algebraically.
2. **Positive engineering magnitudes:** explicitly label powers as supplied into,
   useful out of, non-useful out of, or accumulated inside the boundary.

Both are correct. Mixing them without translating signs is not.

For positive magnitudes in their named directions, energy conservation over a
general interval gives the boundary account

$$
E_\text{supplied}
=E_\text{useful,out}+E_\text{nonuseful,out}
+\Delta E_\text{system}.
$$ {#eq-f05-energy-balance}

When the quantities vary smoothly, its rate form is

$$
P_\text{supplied}(t)
=P_\text{useful,out}(t)+P_\text{nonuseful,out}(t)
+\frac{\mathrm dE_\text{system}}{\mathrm dt}.
$$ {#eq-f05-power-balance}

This is a conservation statement, not an efficiency approximation. The two
outgoing terms count energy that has actually crossed the boundary. Internally,
electrical dissipation $P_D$ converts electrical energy into internal energy; that
energy may accumulate before later crossing the boundary as heat. For a component
whose only internal generation is $P_D$, with no other work or energy output, the
corresponding thermal account is [@bergman2017heat]

$$
P_D=\dot Q_\text{out}+\frac{\mathrm dE_\text{th}}{\mathrm dt}.
$$ {#eq-f05-dissipation-balance}

Counting $P_D$ as an outward loss **and** counting the same temperature-rise
energy in $\Delta E_\text{system}$ would double-count it during warm-up. At thermal
steady state the accumulation vanishes and average internal dissipation equals
average heat transfer out. Non-useful outward transfer may also be light, sound,
vibration, or radiation; none is destroyed energy.

At true steady state, system energy does not change, so the accumulation term is
zero. In periodic steady operation, stored energy may rise and fall within every
cycle while its **cycle-average change** is zero. Startup, shutdown, charging, and
discharging do not permit that simplification. F07 develops the electrical storage
elements; here $\Delta E_\text{system}$ keeps the budget honest without
pre-empting their details.

The three destinations in @fig-f05-energy-boundary keep outward transfer and
internal accumulation visually separate.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-f05-energy-boundary
%%| fig-cap: "An energy account begins with a named boundary. Supplied energy can leave as useful output or non-useful output and can temporarily accumulate inside; only after accumulation is negligible over the chosen interval may the supplied and outgoing average powers be equated."
%%| fig-alt: "A vertical flow enters a system boundary as supplied energy, then branches to useful output, non-useful output such as heat or light, and stored-energy change. A note states that steady or whole-cycle averaging makes the stored-energy change zero, whereas startup and shutdown do not."
flowchart TB
  S["Supplied energy"] --> B["Named system boundary"]
  B --> U["Useful output"]
  B --> L["Non-useful output"]
  B --> A["Stored-energy change"]
  A --> C["Zero only at steady state<br/>or over a complete repeated cycle"]
```

### Worked example: a steady DC power budget

Return to the explicitly illustrative 5.00 V assembly after it has reached steady
DC operation. Ripple is neglected, so the constant terminal products equal their
average powers; a physical switching system would instead require the average of
synchronous instantaneous $v(t)i(t)$ samples. Define all entries below as positive
power magnitudes in their labeled directions. The named boundary and ports appear
in @fig-f05-opening-architecture.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-f05-opening-architecture
%%| fig-cap: "The recurring illustrative system at architecture level. Input power crosses TP_IN into the chosen boundary, then reaches the 3.30 V useful-load port, the 5.00 V indicator branch, or unallocated internal paths. The implementation is deliberately unspecified, so this diagram supports the boundary account but is not a component schematic."
%%| fig-alt: "A 5.00 volt source feeds system test point TP_IN at 120 milliamperes and crosses a system boundary. Three downward branches lead to a 3.30 volt useful load at 100 milliamperes, a 5.00 volt indicator at 10 milliamperes, and unallocated conversion, control, and wiring paths."
flowchart TB
  S["5.00 V source"] -->|"TP_IN: 120 mA"| B["Chosen system boundary"]
  B -->|"TP_OUT: 3.30 V, 100 mA"| U["Useful load"]
  B -->|"5.00 V, 10 mA"| I["Indicator branch"]
  B --> O["Conversion, control,<br/>and wiring paths"]
```

| Account item | Calculation | Power |
|---|---:|---:|
| supplied at TP_IN | $(5.00~\text{V})(120~\text{mA})$ | 0.600 W |
| useful load at TP_OUT | $(3.30~\text{V})(100~\text{mA})$ | 0.330 W |
| indicator branch | $(5.00~\text{V})(10.0~\text{mA})$ | 0.050 W |
| unallocated internal power | $0.600-0.330-0.050$ | 0.220 W |

: Illustrative steady power budget. The values are calculated teaching inputs, not measured evidence. {#tbl-f05-opening-budget}

Table @tbl-f05-opening-budget leaves one quantity unallocated rather than
pretending it has already been localized to a component.

Define the **unallocated power** as

$$
P_\text{unallocated}
=P_\text{supplied}-P_\text{useful}-P_\text{indicator}
=0.220~\text{W}.
$$

It is not automatically “the converter chip's heat.” Leads, contacts, a controller,
and other components inside the boundary may share it. The indicator's 0.050 W may
leave partly as light and partly as heat. More test points or calorimetry are
needed to allocate those paths.

After every path has been assigned, use a distinct **closure residual**

$$
r_\text{closure}=P_\text{supplied}-P_\text{useful}
-P_\text{indicator}-P_\text{unallocated}=0.
$$

In physical work, “zero” means consistent with combined uncertainty and the
chosen timing—not an exact row of displayed digits.

## Efficiency and system boundaries

For a fixed boundary and interval with no net decrease of stored energy, define
**external-input energy efficiency** as

$$
\eta_E\equiv
\frac{E_\text{useful,out}}{E_\text{supplied,in}}.
$$ {#eq-f05-energy-efficiency}

At steady or periodic operation, with matched averaging intervals and no net
stored-energy change, the corresponding average-power ratio is

$$
\eta\equiv
\frac{P_\text{useful,out}}{P_\text{supplied,in}},
\qquad
P_\text{loss}=P_\text{supplied,in}-P_\text{useful,out}.
$$ {#eq-f05-power-efficiency}

Efficiency is dimensionless and is often reported as a percentage. For a complete
boundary using positive supplied and useful magnitudes, with every energy input
included **and no omitted net release of stored energy**, $0\le\eta\le1$. During
discharge, a port ratio can exceed 1—or have zero external input—because stored
energy is another contribution. Report its change separately or include released
energy in the available-energy denominator. An unexplained value above 100 %
therefore points to omitted storage, boundary, timing, reference, or calibration
before it points to new physics.

If only the 3.30 V load is useful, the opening assembly has

$$
\eta_\text{system}
=\frac{0.330~\text{W}}{0.600~\text{W}}=0.550=55.0\%.
$$

If the indicator is required output, the useful ratio becomes
$(0.330+0.050)/0.600=63.3\%$. Neither answer is meaningful without the boundary
and definition of useful output. Efficiency is also not voltage regulation,
efficacy in lumens per watt, battery utilization, or maximum power transfer.

For cascaded stages with matched boundaries and operating intervals,

$$
\eta_\text{total}
=\frac{P_2}{P_0}
=\frac{P_1}{P_0}\frac{P_2}{P_1}
=\eta_1\eta_2.
$$ {#eq-f05-cascade-efficiency}

Two 90 % stages therefore give 81 %, not 80 % and not 90 %. The product is invalid
if $P_1$ is counted at different test points or time intervals, or if an auxiliary
rail bypasses the stage boundary.

### Linear conversion and dissipation

Consider an illustrative requirement: deliver 5.00 V DC at 100 mA from 12.0 V DC.
For an idealized **linear regulator**, define input current as output current plus
ground current $I_G$. Its steady input, output, and internal powers are

$$
P_\text{in}=V_\text{in}(I_\text{out}+I_G),
\qquad
P_\text{out}=V_\text{out}I_\text{out},
$$

$$
P_\text{loss}
=(V_\text{in}-V_\text{out})I_\text{out}+V_\text{in}I_G.
$$ {#eq-f05-linear-loss}

With the explicitly illustrative $I_G=2.0$ mA,

$$
P_\text{in}=1.224~\text{W},\quad
P_\text{out}=0.500~\text{W},\quad
P_\text{loss}=0.724~\text{W},\quad
\eta=40.8\%.
$$

The shortcut $\eta\approx V_\text{out}/V_\text{in}=41.7\%$ is the limiting case
$I_G\ll I_\text{out}$; it is not exact here. As $V_\text{in}$ rises at fixed load,
linear-regulator loss rises. A switching architecture can reduce that loss, but
its efficiency varies with load, input voltage, switching frequency, and
implementation and must come from a specified device and operating condition.
F05 supplies the accounting; [S05](../05-domains/s05-power-electronics.qmd)
develops the conversion mechanisms and trade-offs.

## From electrical dissipation to temperature rise

Temperature, stored internal energy, and heat are not synonyms. **Temperature** is
a thermodynamic state variable. **Heat** is energy crossing a boundary because of
a temperature difference. A component can accumulate internal energy while its
temperature rises; at thermal steady state its temperature stops rising even
though heat continues to flow out at the same average rate that power is dissipated
inside [@bergman2017heat].

Choose a hot point at temperature $T_H$ and a reference point at $T_R$. Define heat
flow rate $\dot Q$ as positive from the hot point toward the reference. A commonly
useful **linearized, steady-state constitutive approximation** defines thermal
resistance between those named points as

$$
R_\theta\equiv\frac{T_H-T_R}{\dot Q},
\qquad [R_\theta]=\text{K/W}.
$$ {#eq-f05-thermal-resistance-definition}

If essentially all component dissipation $P_D$ follows the characterized path and
conditions are steady,

$$
\Delta T\equiv T_H-T_R\approx P_D R_\theta,
\qquad
T_H\approx T_R+P_D R_\theta.
$$ {#eq-f05-steady-temperature}

A temperature **difference** of 1 K equals a difference of 1 °C, so K/W and °C/W
have the same numerical slope. Absolute temperatures are not interchangeable in
relations such as thermal radiation, which requires kelvin.

Thermal resistance is not an intrinsic promise detached from geometry. To see why,
consider one-dimensional conduction through a uniform slab of thickness $L$, area
$A$, and approximately constant thermal conductivity $k$. Fourier conduction is
the constitutive relation

$$
\dot Q=kA\frac{T_H-T_C}{L},
$$

for steady heat flow from hot to cold. Comparing with
@eq-f05-thermal-resistance-definition gives

$$
R_{\theta,\text{cond}}=\frac{L}{kA}.
$$ {#eq-f05-conduction-resistance}

Its units are
$(\text{m})/[\text{W}/(\text{m}\cdot\text{K})\,\text{m}^2]=\text{K/W}$.
Doubling thickness doubles resistance; doubling cross-sectional area halves it;
$k\to\infty$ approaches an isothermal link. Spreading, interfaces, contact
pressure, nonuniform area, temperature-dependent conductivity, convection, and
radiation break the one-dimensional picture [@bergman2017heat].

Series thermal resistances add only when essentially the same heat flow traverses
them in sequence. Simultaneous routes through leads, package, board, air, and a heat
sink act in parallel. Radiation is intrinsically nonlinear, while convection's
effective coefficient is condition-dependent even though Newton cooling is linear
in temperature difference when that coefficient is held constant. A single
$R_\theta$ is therefore normally a local engineering approximation around stated
power, temperatures, orientation, enclosure, and airflow.

### Semiconductor package thermal metrics

For an IC, $R_{\theta JA}$ denotes a junction-to-ambient result under specified
package, test-board, mounting, and air conditions. It is not a portable package
constant. Board copper, thermal vias, die size, power, nearby heat sources,
orientation, enclosure, and airflow can change the application result. A
manufacturer's $\Psi_{JT}$ is a characterization parameter for estimating junction
temperature from package-top temperature under its defined conditions; it is not
interchangeable with $R_{\theta JC}$. Nor should a complete $R_{\theta JA}$ path be
added to a separate junction-to-case plus heat-sink path—the same heat routes would
be counted twice. The distinctions and test dependence are documented in the TI
package-thermal-metrics report [@ti2024thermalmetrics].

Thus

$$
T_J\approx T_A+P_D R_{\theta JA}
$$

is at most a screening estimate unless the application is sufficiently similar to
the characterization conditions or an application-specific effective resistance
has been established. A **typical** thermal metric cannot become a guaranteed
worst-case bound by arithmetic. When a manufacturer supplies $\Psi_{JT}$ for the
relevant conditions, the distinct estimate

$$
T_J\approx T_\text{top}+\Psi_{JT}P_D
$$

uses a measured package-top temperature; it does not turn $\Psi_{JT}$ into a
series thermal resistance or remove the measurement and condition uncertainties
[@ti2024thermalmetrics].

## Thermal capacitance and transient temperature

::: {.callout-note title="Optional bridge to first-order dynamics"}
Using the step-response relation and interpreting its limits require only algebra,
an exponential function, and the idea of a time constant. The differential-equation
derivation is an optional bridge to [M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd)
and [F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd); readers following only the F03
prerequisite may use the stated response here and return to the derivation later.
:::

Let one approximately uniform thermal mass have temperature $T(t)$, reference
ambient $T_A$, and thermal capacitance $C_\theta$ in J/K. Define $C_\theta$ by the
incremental stored-energy relation
$\mathrm dE_\text{th}=C_\theta\,\mathrm dT$. With constant internal dissipation
$P_D$ and a linear heat path $R_\theta$, conservation gives

$$
C_\theta\frac{\mathrm dT}{\mathrm dt}
=P_D-\frac{T-T_A}{R_\theta}.
$$ {#eq-f05-thermal-balance}

This is a **single-node, first-order approximation**. For a power step applied at
$t=0$ from initial condition $T(0)=T_A$, the solution is

$$
T(t)=T_A+P_D R_\theta
\left(1-e^{-t/\tau_\theta}\right),
\qquad
\tau_\theta=R_\theta C_\theta.
$$ {#eq-f05-thermal-step}

Substitution verifies the solution: at $t=0$, the rise is zero and its initial
slope is $P_D/C_\theta$; as $t\to\infty$, the exponential vanishes and
@eq-f05-steady-temperature returns. Dimensions also agree:
$(\text{K/W})(\text{J/K})=\text{J/W}=\text{s}$. The calculus support is in
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd); F07 develops the same
accumulation-versus-flow structure for electrical transients.

Real packages usually have several thermal masses and paths, so one exponential
may fit only part of the record. Power-dependent convection and radiation,
temperature-dependent dissipation, changing airflow, and neighboring heat sources
also make $R_\theta$ or $C_\theta$ appear to change.

### Worked example: first-order thermal settling

For this separate synthetic scenario only, assume the entire 0.220 W unallocated
system power is dissipated in one component and follows the represented heat path.
Take illustrative parameters
$T_A=23.0~^\circ\text{C}$, $P_D=0.220$ W,
$R_\theta=120$ K/W, and $C_\theta=0.750$ J/K. The predicted steady rise is
26.4 K, the final temperature is 49.4 °C, and
$\tau_\theta=90.0$ s.

| Time after step (s) | Temperature (°C) |
|---:|---:|
| 0 | 23.0 |
| 30 | 30.5 |
| 60 | 35.8 |
| 120 | 42.4 |
| 240 | 47.6 |
| 360 | 48.9 |
| steady limit | 49.4 |

: **Synthetic illustrative data** generated from @eq-f05-thermal-step, rounded to 0.1 °C. They teach settling and residual checks but do not qualify any device or assembly. {#tbl-f05-synthetic-temperature}

At $t=60$ s the part is still 13.6 K below the steady prediction. Treating that
reading as steady would underestimate rise by more than half. A physical test needs
an equilibrium criterion such as a slope small enough for a specified duration,
not merely “wait one minute.”

The following standard-library program closes the opening budget and generates the
same thermal prediction. Parameters are visible so that boundary or thermal
assumptions can be changed deliberately.

```python
from math import exp

def power_w(voltage_v, current_a):
    return voltage_v * current_a

def temperature_c(time_s, ambient_c, loss_w,
                  thermal_k_per_w, thermal_j_per_k):
    tau_s = thermal_k_per_w * thermal_j_per_k
    rise_k = loss_w * thermal_k_per_w
    return ambient_c + rise_k * (1.0 - exp(-time_s / tau_s))

pin_w = power_w(5.00, 0.120)
pload_w = power_w(3.30, 0.100)
pindicator_w = power_w(5.00, 0.0100)
unallocated_w = pin_w - pload_w - pindicator_w
# Separate synthetic assumption: all unallocated power heats one part.
assumed_component_loss_w = unallocated_w

print(f"input       {pin_w:.3f} W")
print(f"useful      {pload_w:.3f} W")
print(f"indicator   {pindicator_w:.3f} W")
print(f"unallocated {unallocated_w:.3f} W")
print(f"efficiency  {100 * pload_w / pin_w:.1f} %")
for time_s in (0, 30, 60, 120, 240, 360):
    temp_c = temperature_c(
        time_s, 23.0, assumed_component_loss_w, 120.0, 0.750
    )
    print(f"T({time_s:3d} s)    {temp_c:.1f} degC")
```

Expected output:

```text
input       0.600 W
useful      0.330 W
indicator   0.050 W
unallocated 0.220 W
efficiency  55.0 %
T(  0 s)    23.0 degC
T( 30 s)    30.5 degC
T( 60 s)    35.8 degC
T(120 s)    42.4 degC
T(240 s)    47.6 degC
T(360 s)    48.9 degC
```

This execution reproduces an analytical calculation; it is neither a device
simulation nor a measurement. Concentrating all unallocated power in one part is
an explicit, testable hypothesis, not an observed fact.

## Power ratings, derating, and simultaneous limits

A component rating answers a specific question under stated conditions. No single
number replaces the others:

| Datasheet item | Question it constrains | Common mistake |
|---|---|---|
| continuous power | average dissipation under stated mounting and ambient | treating package size as a universal wattage |
| derating curve | permitted power as ambient or case temperature changes | applying the room-temperature value everywhere |
| maximum temperature | film, junction, case, or ambient limit at a named point | substituting an easier surface measurement |
| operating voltage | electrical stress independent of average power | checking only $V^2/R$ |
| pulse/overload curve | peak power or energy for a stated duration and repetition | assuming low average power makes any pulse safe |
| tolerance and TCR | value spread and temperature response | treating them as measurement uncertainty |
| absolute maximum | boundary beyond which damage may occur | using equality as a recommended operating point |

Ratings can interact. In a positive-TCR resistor under constant current,
$P=I^2R(T)$ rises as resistance rises, reinforcing heating. Under constant voltage,
$P=V^2/R(T)$ falls for the same positive TCR. Semiconductors can show stronger
electrothermal feedback and thermal runaway. A constant-$R$ calculation is a first
screen, not permission to ignore temperature dependence.

The authentic Vishay MRS25 datasheet illustrates the conditional language. For
that specified series it lists a 0.6 W rated dissipation at 70 °C, 350 V maximum
AC/DC operating voltage, −55 °C to 155 °C operating range, and 155 °C peak
permissible film temperature. It also states that resistor temperature rise
depends on PCB heat-flow support, that the rated dissipation applies only if the
film-temperature limit is respected, and that long-term resistance drift can set
a functional lifetime [@vishaymrs25]. Those facts are manufacturer document
evidence, not measurements of a particular board. The 350 V value is far outside
this chapter's practical safety boundary and conveys no authorization to operate
there.

For any manufacturer curve or table, define $P_\text{permitted}(T_A)$ only under
its stated mounting and environment. A continuous-power screen then requires

$$
P_{D,\max}<P_\text{permitted}(T_{A,\max}),
$$

in addition to—not instead of—voltage, pulse, film/junction-temperature, and
lifetime checks. For the worked design that follows, the cited datasheet supplies
$P_\text{permitted}(70~^\circ\text{C})=0.600$ W [@vishaymrs25]; the application
power must still be bounded before the comparison can be made. At an ambient above
70 °C, this chapter does not invent an interpolation: the current manufacturer
derating curve and its conditions must be read directly. If no guaranteed curve
or application-specific thermal bound is available, the result remains a
feasibility screen rather than a worst-case pass.

## Worked design: resistor power and margin

The claim is intentionally limited to terminal power. It does **not** accept film
or junction temperature because the fixture described here cannot observe that
measurand or establish a qualified correlation from surface temperature.

Consider this deliberately low-voltage design requirement:

- source at the resistor terminals: $5.00$ V DC nominal, bounded from 4.90 V to
  5.10 V in operation;
- resistor: MRS25, 100 Ω nominal at the 20 °C design reference,
  ±1 % initial tolerance and
  ±50 ppm/K TCR magnitude [@vishaymrs25];
- local ambient: 20 °C to 70 °C, still-air orientation and board mounting to be
  recorded;
- internal continuous-power design limit: **strictly less than 0.300 W**, chosen
  below the datasheet's 0.6 W rating at 70 °C;
- steady surface temperature is a diagnostic measurand, while film temperature is
  acknowledged as inaccessible in this setup; and
- no pulse or overload operation.

At constant voltage, maximum resistance power occurs at maximum voltage and
minimum resistance. As a first-order bound, conservatively allow the signed TCR to
reduce resistance from a 20 °C reference to the 155 °C permitted-film boundary.
This is wider than the ambient range because self-heating makes film temperature
exceed ambient. It
treats initial tolerance and temperature shift as simultaneous bounds, not as
probability distributions:

$$
R_{\min}=(100~\Omega)(1-0.01)
\left[1-(50\times10^{-6}~\text{K}^{-1})(135~\text{K})\right]
=98.33~\Omega,
$$

$$
P_{R,\max}
=\frac{(5.10~\text{V})^2}{98.33~\Omega}
=0.265~\text{W}.
$$ {#eq-f05-resistor-worst-power}

The units reduce to $\text{V}^2/\Omega=\text{W}$. Nominal power would be only
0.250 W; carrying source and resistance bounds consumes about 15 mW of the apparent
50 mW margin. The worst-case calculation leaves
$0.300-0.265=0.035$ W to the internal design limit and remains below the
datasheet's 0.6 W rating at the stated 70 °C reference condition.

The calculation-to-datasheet comparison is therefore
$0.265~\text{W}<0.600~\text{W}$: 44.2 % of the permitted dissipation at the cited
reference condition. The 0.265 W value is a bounded chapter calculation; the
0.600 W value is manufacturer document evidence [@vishaymrs25].

The simultaneous voltage screen is also satisfied:
$5.10~\text{V}<350~\text{V}$ for this series [@vishaymrs25]. This comparison is
not authorization to approach 350 V; it shows that the low-voltage design is
governed by power and thermal conditions rather than the separate operating-voltage
rating.

**Decision:** conditionally proceed for the strict terminal-power requirement,
because the bounded 0.265 W result is below 0.300 W. This establishes
**document-based electrical feasibility under the stated bounds**, not verified
film temperature, lifetime, pulse capability, or production qualification. The
datasheet explicitly makes PCB heat flow relevant, and a case
or lacquer-surface reading is not the film hot spot. A qualified product decision
needs the current manufacturer documentation, actual mounting, environmental
tests, drift requirements, and evidence appropriate to the consequence of failure.
Turning the diagnostic surface record into a thermal acceptance rule would require
a manufacturer-supported or independently validated correlation to film
temperature for the actual mounting, with its own calibration and uncertainty.

### Acceptance test for terminal power

For an approved L1 fixture only, a defensible acceptance test for the narrower
continuous-power requirement would:

1. identify the resistor and mounting; record local ambient, board, enclosure,
   orientation, airflow, source current limit, and stabilization criterion;
2. measure DC voltage directly at the resistor terminals and current in the same
   steady interval, with declared polarities and instrument ranges;
3. account for voltmeter loading, ammeter burden, lead/contact loss, accuracy
   limits, resolution, calibration state, repeatability, and temperature drift in
   the uncertainty basis [@jcgm2008gum];
4. calculate $P_\text{meas}=V_R I_R$ and a conservative power guard $g_P$ at the
   stated coverage; do not relabel a datasheet accuracy limit as expanded
   uncertainty;
5. pass the strict electrical requirement only if
   $P_\text{meas}+g_P<0.300$ W at every required operating corner—equality does
   not pass; and
6. record ambient and resistor-surface temperature versus time as diagnostic
   evidence, but do not claim the unmeasured film temperature from the surface
   reading alone.

The thermal observation can reveal instability, inadequate settling, or mounting
changes. It cannot strengthen the claim beyond the measurand and uncertainty that
the setup actually supports.

## Reconciling power and temperature evidence

Suppose, in a **hypothetical illustrative test rather than a measurement record**,
the opening assembly's input appears to be
0.600 W while the 0.330 W useful output and 0.050 W indicator add to 0.380 W, yet
a temperature estimate that assigns all 0.220 W of unallocated power to one part
is far above its observed surface temperature. Do not immediately conclude that
energy conservation or the temperature sensor has
failed. Competing hypotheses include:

- the power readings were taken at different times or operating modes;
- an auxiliary output or stored-energy change was omitted;
- source-display values were used instead of simultaneous voltage at the load
  terminals and branch current;
- wiring or ammeter burden moved a test point outside the intended boundary;
- the part had not reached thermal steady state;
- heat leaves through multiple paths, so the assumed $R_\theta$ is wrong;
- surface temperature is below the internal hot spot, or the sensor attachment,
  emissivity, or ambient reference is biased [@ti2024thermalmetrics]; and
- dissipation is distributed among several components rather than concentrated in
  the observed one.

A useful diagnostic sequence is:

1. redraw the boundary and inventory every crossing power path;
2. sample input and outputs over the same interval and calculate a residual with
   uncertainty;
3. measure voltage at both ends of suspect leads to localize electrical loss;
4. continue the thermal record until a quantitative slope criterion is met;
5. change one heat-flow condition, such as controlled airflow, without changing
   electrical load, and observe which temperatures respond; and
6. compare the complete time record with one- and multi-time-constant predictions.

This is the chapter's working cycle: **predict → build → measure → reconcile →
decide** [@horowitz2015art]. Calculation can establish a feasibility screen.
Synthetic data can test analysis. Manufacturer documents can establish
conditional ratings. Only a traceable physical test reaches evidence about the
actual assembly, and repeated
or qualified tests are required when the consequence warrants them.

## Summary

- With current defined into the voltage-positive terminal,
  $p_\text{abs}(t)=v(t)i(t)$; positive means absorption and negative delivery.
- Energy is the time integral of power, and average power is energy divided by a
  named interval. Mode-weighted budgets sum $P_kt_k$; in general, average $vi$ is
  not average $v$ times average $i$.
- A complete boundary account includes useful and non-useful outward transfer plus
  system-energy change. Internal dissipation becomes outward heat only after any
  thermal accumulation is accounted for.
- Efficiency is a ratio only after the useful output, boundary, operating mode,
  and interval are fixed. Cascaded efficiencies multiply across matched ports.
- Dissipation and heat transfer are linked by a condition-dependent thermal path.
  $\Delta T\approx P_D R_\theta$ is a steady, linearized approximation, while
  thermal capacitance explains why temperature takes time to settle.
- Power, voltage, pulse, temperature, mounting, and lifetime ratings apply
  together. Equality to a strict limit is not margin, and a typical thermal metric
  is not a guaranteed application bound.
- A defensible decision names the measurand, test points, environment, instrument
  loading, uncertainty, guard band, and explicit pass/fail rule.

## Exercises

### Quick check

Choose one best answer for each question.

1. Under the passive sign convention, a component has $v=+5$ V and
   $i=-20$ mA. It is:
    a. absorbing 100 mW;
    b. delivering 100 mW;
    c. storing exactly 100 mJ;
    d. violating conservation.
2. Which quantity is an amount of energy?
    a. 2 W;
    b. 2 A;
    c. 2 Wh;
    d. 2 K/W.
3. In periodic steady operation, stored energy may:
    a. never change within a cycle;
    b. change within a cycle while its whole-cycle net change is zero;
    c. increase by the same amount every cycle forever;
    d. be omitted from every instantaneous balance.
4. A reported converter efficiency is meaningful only when:
    a. output voltage alone is known;
    b. input and useful output use a stated boundary, mode, and matched interval;
    c. it is below 100 %;
    d. the package remains cool to touch.
5. A datasheet value of $R_{\theta JA}$ is best interpreted as:
    a. an intrinsic constant independent of PCB and airflow;
    b. electrical resistance from junction to ambient;
    c. a thermal result tied to stated package and test conditions;
    d. the same quantity as $\Psi_{JT}$.
6. A worst-case prediction equals a strict maximum rating. The design:
    a. passes with zero uncertainty;
    b. passes because equality is conservative;
    c. needs no thermal test;
    d. does not pass the strict requirement because it has no margin.

*[Key: 1-b, 2-c, 3-b, 4-b, 5-c, 6-d.]*

### Retrieval and explanation

1. Define energy, instantaneous power, average power, dissipation, efficiency,
   heat, temperature, thermal resistance, and thermal capacitance. Give the SI
   unit of each.
2. State the passive sign convention and explain why negative absorbed power is
   not an error.
3. Explain why “the resistor consumed 15 W” is ambiguous or wrong. Rewrite it for
   (a) a rate and (b) an amount over 60 s.
4. Explain why an ampere-hour rating is not an energy rating and why nominal
   voltage times ampere-hours is only an estimate of usable energy.
5. Distinguish junction, case, surface, local ambient, and room temperature. Why
   can no one value silently replace another?

### Estimation

6. Use @eq-f05-mode-budget for a 5 V system that draws 20 mA in standby and
   200 mA while active for 10 % of each hour. Estimate average input power and
   energy per day before calculating. State what transition energy the two-mode
   calculation omits.
   *[Self-check: 0.190 W and 4.56 Wh/day.]*
7. A converter supplies 2.0 W at 80 % efficiency. Estimate input power and loss.
   Then repeat at 95 %. Which improvement matters more thermally?
8. A component dissipates 0.50 W with an application-specific thermal resistance
   near 60 K/W at 25 °C ambient. Estimate its hot-point temperature. State why
   the result is not yet a worst-case bound. *[Self-check: about 55 °C.]*
9. An illustrative battery stores 12 Wh, of which 75 % is usable through the
   converter. Estimate runtime for a constant 1.5 W load. Name two effects that
   could invalidate the estimate. *[Self-check: about 6 h.]*

### Derivation and calculation

10. Starting from $\mathrm dW=v\,\mathrm dq$ and
    $i=\mathrm dq/\mathrm dt$, derive @eq-f05-instantaneous-power. Declare the
    references and show units.
11. Reverse only the current reference in the 5.00 V, 100 Ω source-load example.
    Write a correct power relation in the new variables and show that the physical
    source and load transfers are unchanged.
12. Derive @eq-f05-resistor-power from $v=iR$. State why it guarantees
    nonnegative absorbed power only for a positive ohmic resistance under the
    passive sign convention.
13. A 12 V pulse is applied to 48 Ω for 2.0 ms every 10 ms. Calculate peak power,
    duty cycle, average power, energy per pulse, $V_\text{avg}$, $I_\text{avg}$,
    $V_\text{rms}$, and $I_\text{rms}$. Verify that average $vi$ differs from
    $V_\text{avg}I_\text{avg}$.
14. Derive @eq-f05-cascade-efficiency for three stages. Calculate total efficiency
    for 92 %, 85 %, and 96 % stages and the required input for 4.0 W useful output.
15. **Extension.** Verify @eq-f05-thermal-step by substituting it into
    @eq-f05-thermal-balance. Interpret the limits $t=0$, $t=\tau_\theta$, and
    $t\to\infty$.
16. **Extension.** Starting from one-dimensional Fourier conduction, derive
    @eq-f05-conduction-resistance. Predict the effect of doubling $L$, $A$, and
    $k$ separately.

### Data interpretation

17. Recalculate every finite row of @tbl-f05-synthetic-temperature from
    @eq-f05-thermal-step. At what time is the temperature within 5 % of its final
    rise? Why can this synthetic agreement not qualify hardware?
18. An illustrative test record (not physical evidence) gives
    $V_\text{in}=5.01$ V and $I_\text{in}=118$ mA,
    $V_\text{out}=3.29$ V and $I_\text{out}=101$ mA, plus a 49 mW auxiliary
    branch. Calculate input, useful, unallocated power, and useful efficiency.
    Name the uncertainty information needed before localizing the unallocated
    power to a component.
19. A part dissipating 0.20, 0.40, and 0.60 W reaches steady rises of 18, 33, and
    45 K in a synthetic illustrative record. Calculate apparent $R_\theta$ at each
    point. Does one constant thermal resistance fit? Suggest two physical
    explanations.
20. An illustrative thermal trace continues rising at 0.4 K/min when the record
    stops. Explain why its last temperature is not a steady-state result and
    propose a quantitative stabilization rule.

### Debugging

21. A power budget reports 110 % efficiency during startup. Give four hypotheses
    involving stored energy, timing, test points, sign, or omitted inputs, and one
    measurement that discriminates at least two of them.
22. Two instruments report different input power for a pulsed load. One multiplies
    displayed average voltage by displayed average current; the other averages
    simultaneous waveform products. Which method targets average real power, and
    under what bandwidth and sampling conditions could it still fail?
23. An IC runs hotter after the same design is moved to a smaller PCB, although
    electrical dissipation is unchanged. Explain why copying the earlier
    $R_{\theta JA}$ result is invalid. Name evidence that would separate changed
    copper heat spreading from changed airflow.
24. An infrared camera reads a package cooler than a fine thermocouple. Propose
    hypotheses involving emissivity, reflection, sensor attachment, spatial
    resolution, and sensor heat sinking. Do not assume either is the truth standard.
25. A resistor meets its average-power rating but fails after repetitive pulses.
    Identify at least four unchecked constraints and state what manufacturer data
    would be needed.

### Open design

26. Choose between a linear and switching architecture for 12 V to 5 V at loads
    from 5 mA to 300 mA. Set requirements for regulation, standby current,
    efficiency at multiple loads, ripple, cost, and maximum temperature. Use real
    current datasheets, calculate worst-case losses, and state what physical tests
    remain.
27. Design an approved extra-low-voltage L04 experiment that compares predicted
    resistor power with temperature rise. Specify resistor identity and ratings,
    voltage/current references, current limit, test points, ambient/airflow and
    mounting, temperature measurand and attachment, instruments/ranges/calibration,
    raw time records, uncertainty, stop conditions, and a guarded decision rule.
28. Develop a 24-hour energy budget for a battery-powered sensor with sleep,
    sampling, processing, radio transmit, and conversion losses. State every duty
    cycle, include standby paths, carry battery usable-energy uncertainty, and
    identify the mode that dominates energy rather than peak power.
29. Select a real resistor for the bounded case in this chapter. Use the current
    manufacturer datasheet to check continuous derating, operating voltage,
    pulse/overload limits, TCR, mounting, drift, and availability. Define a design
    margin and state exactly what document review proves and what needs physical
    qualification.

## Connections

Chapter ID: `F05`. Its registered prerequisite is
[F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd), which supplies associated references,
source-load operating points, resistance, loading, and ratings. Algebra and SI
support are in [M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd); the differential
equation in the optional thermal-settling bridge is supported by
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd).

Direct downstream chapters are [F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd),
which develops electrical energy storage and first-order transients;
[A01](../02-analog/a01-semiconductor-diodes.qmd) and
[A02](../02-analog/a02-transistors.qmd), which add temperature-sensitive device
regions and dissipation; and [S05](../05-domains/s05-power-electronics.qmd), which
develops efficient conversion. [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd)
provides the full uncertainty and fault-isolation machinery assumed by the
acceptance rule.

The aligned physical evidence is **Lab L04 — Power, efficiency, and temperature
rise**, at safety level L1. The chapter also contributes to **M1 — Measure an
energy-and-light system**. Reproducible configuration and raw-data practice connect
to [T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd), and datasheet
interpretation to [T01](../appendices/t01-datasheets-standards.qmd). These IDs
denote learning and evidence goals; they do not authorize practical work. The
[Part I introduction](index.qmd) and [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) show the
full route.

## References

Claims are cited where used. SI definitions follow the SI Brochure
[@bipm2019si]; circuit power conventions and practical electronics treatment
follow canonical electronics texts [@sedra2020microelectronic;
@horowitz2015art]. Heat-flow relations follow the canonical heat-transfer
treatment [@bergman2017heat]. Package thermal-metric limits follow the
manufacturer application report [@ti2024thermalmetrics], and the authentic
resistor example follows the MRS16/MRS25 manufacturer datasheet
[@vishaymrs25]. Measurement-uncertainty language follows [@jcgm2008gum]. The
complete [bibliography](../references.qmd) collects these sources. No textbook,
datasheet, calculation, or chapter substitutes for local authorization or
qualification of a physical design.
